quote: Existing decompilers can reconstruct much of the original source code, but typically use meaningless placeholder variables
The paper argues that original names are lost but natural names can be assigned by learning from similar contexts.
DIRE uses lexical and structural information from decompiler output and reports up to 74.3 percent exact original-name prediction.
DIRTY predicts names and types from decompiled files using context and reports 66.4 percent name and 75.8 percent type recovery.
VarBERT takes raw decompiler output and reports 84.15 percent exact original-name prediction on a large dataset.
STRIDE predicts decompiled variable names and types by matching decompiler-token sequences to training data.
CodeSearchNet provides datasets, baseline models, metrics, and benchmark infrastructure for semantic code search.
CodeBERT learns general representations for natural-language and programming-language tasks including code search.
we use data flow in the pre-training stage
CodeT5 introduces identifier-aware pretraining and uses comments for natural-language/programming-language alignment.
UniXcoder leverages AST and code comments to enhance code representation and evaluates code search and code-to-code search.
learns code functionality, not form
code2vec represents code using AST paths and learns vectors that predict semantic properties such as method names.
code2seq uses AST paths and attention for summarization, documentation, and retrieval tasks.
Qdrant recommends natural-language and code-specific embeddings, plus function/method-sized chunks enriched with metadata.
split the code by AST nodes
Elastic describes hybrid retrieval as lexical, semantic, and fusion stages, combining exact terms with meaning-based search.
Qdrant shows sparse and dense prefetches fused with reciprocal rank fusion in a hybrid query.
Cody separates retrieval and ranking, optimizes retrieval for recall, and constrains ranking by relevance and token budget.
Cursor splits changed files into syntactic chunks, embeds asynchronously, and caches embeddings by chunk content.
HyDE generates a hypothetical document from a query, embeds it, and retrieves real documents in the nearby embedding space.
The codebase RAG example uses LLM-generated comments, hybrid search, cross-encoder reranking, and HyDE.
